lessons.title: Date Types: year_month_day, Arithmetic, hh_mm_ss

background.title: std::chrono: duration and time_point (C++11)
background.content: The `<chrono>` library was introduced in C++11. `std::chrono::duration<Rep, Period>` represents a time interval (e.g. `seconds`, `milliseconds`). `std::chrono::time_point<Clock, Duration>` represents a point in time relative to a clock's epoch. C++20 built a full calendar system on top of this foundation: types for year, month, day, and timezone support.

lessons.motivation: Before C++20, working with dates required third-party libraries (Boost.Date_Time, Howard Hinnant's date.h) or manual calculations through `time_t` / `struct tm` â€” with endless bugs around month-end boundaries, leap years, and timezones. C++20 brings this into the standard: the date `2026y/July/21d` reads like natural language, arithmetic (`ymd + months{3}`) handles month-end transitions automatically, and `year_month_day::ok()` validates a date instantly.
lessons.theory:
## Literals and year_month_day

C++20 introduces suffixes for creating date components directly in code:

```cpp
using namespace std::chrono;
using namespace std::chrono_literals;

auto y = 2026y;          // std::chrono::year{2026}
auto m = July;           // std::chrono::month{7}, constant in namespace chrono
auto d = 21d;            // std::chrono::day{21}
```

`year_month_day` stores a year, month, and day as a single value. It's conveniently created via the `/` operator:

```cpp
year_month_day ymd = 2026y/July/21d;           // year/month/day order
year_month_day ymd2{year{2026}, month{7}, day{21}};  // explicit constructor
```

Formatting uses `std::format` with chrono format specifiers:

```cpp
std::format("{:%Y-%m-%d}", ymd)   // "2026-07-21"
std::format("{:%d %B %Y}", ymd)   // "21 July 2026"
```

## Date Arithmetic

You can add `months` and `years` to `year_month_day`; the result correctly handles month-end transitions:

```cpp
auto ymd = 2026y/July/21d;
auto next_month = ymd + months{1};   // 2026-08-21
auto next_year  = ymd + years{1};    // 2027-07-21
```

For day-level arithmetic, convert through `sys_days`:

```cpp
sys_days sd{ymd};              // time_point<system_clock, days>
auto tomorrow = sd + days{1};  // arithmetic works on days
year_month_day tomorrow_ymd{tomorrow};  // convert back to calendar type
```

## Last Day of Month and weekday

`std::chrono::last` is a special marker for "the last day of the month":

```cpp
auto feb_last = 2026y/February/last;       // year_month_day_last
year_month_day feb_last_ymd{feb_last};     // 2026-02-28
```

`std::chrono::weekday` represents a day of the week (0 = Sunday, 6 = Saturday). Constants `Sunday`â€¦`Saturday` are defined in namespace `std::chrono`:

```cpp
sys_days sd{2026y/July/21d};
weekday wd{sd};               // computed from date -> sys_days
std::format("{}", wd);        // "Tue"
```

## Validation: ymd.ok()

`year_month_day::ok()` returns `true` if the date exists in the real calendar:

```cpp
year_month_day valid{2026y/July/21d};       // ok() == true
year_month_day invalid{2026y/February/30d}; // ok() == false (Feb has no 30th)
```

This is useful when parsing user input â€” create the object and check immediately.

## hh_mm_ss: Breaking Down a Duration

`std::chrono::hh_mm_ss<Duration>` splits any `duration` into hours, minutes, seconds, and subseconds:

```cpp
auto d = 3h + 45min + 30s + 123ms;
hh_mm_ss hms{d};
hms.hours().count()      // 3
hms.minutes().count()    // 45
hms.seconds().count()    // 30
hms.subseconds().count() // 123  (type is milliseconds because the input Duration is ms)
```

The subsecond type is automatically derived from the precision of the input `Duration`. Pass `nanoseconds` and `subseconds()` will return `nanoseconds`.

lessons.title: Timezones: time_zone, zoned_time, Conversion
lessons.motivation: Without standard timezone support, developers either hardcoded UTC offsets or pulled in third-party libraries that could go stale as the IANA database evolved. C++20 includes full IANA timezone database integration: a single call to `locate_zone("America/New_York")` gives you an object that knows the complete DST transition history. `zoned_time` stores a time point paired with a timezone and automatically converts between them when formatting.
lessons.theory:
## The IANA Database and get_tzdb()

C++20 requires implementations to provide access to the IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) timezone database â€” the same one used in Linux's `/usr/share/zoneinfo`. Access it via:

```cpp
const auto& db = std::chrono::get_tzdb();  // tzdb&
std::cout << db.version;  // e.g. "2026b"
```

`get_tzdb()` returns a reference to a global singleton containing all timezone definitions and their historical UTC offset changes.

## locate_zone and time_zone

```cpp
const std::chrono::time_zone* tz = std::chrono::locate_zone("Europe/Berlin");
```

If the name is not found, `std::runtime_error` is thrown. Name strings follow IANA conventions: "America/New_York", "Asia/Tokyo", "UTC", "Europe/Berlin", etc.

## zoned_time

`std::chrono::zoned_time<Duration>` is a pair: a system time (`sys_time<Duration>`) plus a pointer to a `time_zone`. Construct it from a zone and a `sys_time`:

```cpp
using namespace std::chrono;
sys_days sd{year{2026}/March/8d};
auto tp = sd + hours{12};           // 2026-03-08 12:00:00 UTC

const time_zone* utc = locate_zone("UTC");
zoned_time<seconds> zt{utc, tp};    // bind the moment to a timezone
```

When formatting, `%Z` gives the timezone abbreviation and `%H:%M:%S` gives the local time:

```cpp
std::format("{:%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S %Z}", zt)  // "2026-03-08 12:00:00 UTC"
```

Converting between zones is simply constructing a new `zoned_time` from an existing one:

```cpp
const time_zone* ny = locate_zone("America/New_York");
zoned_time<seconds> zt_ny{ny, zt};  // same moment, different timezone
```

## Daylight Saving Time (DST)

`zoned_time` handles DST transitions automatically. On March 8, 2026, US clocks spring forward: 2:00 AM â†’ 3:00 AM. The moment 6:30 UTC = 1:30 EST (before transition); 8:00 UTC = 4:00 EDT (after transition):

```cpp
// 6:30 UTC -> 1:30 EST (Eastern Standard Time, UTC-5)
zoned_time<minutes> before{ny, sd + hours{6} + minutes{30}};
// 8:00 UTC -> 4:00 EDT (Eastern Daylight Time, UTC-4)
zoned_time<minutes> after{ny, sd + hours{8}};
```

The `%Z` abbreviation changes automatically: EST before the transition, EDT after.

## clock_cast: Converting Between Clock Types

C++20 introduces several system clocks with different epochs:

| Type | Epoch | Notes |
|------|-------|-------|
| `system_clock` | 1970-01-01 UTC | Can jump (NTP adjustments) |
| `utc_clock` | 1970-01-01 UTC | Counts leap seconds |
| `tai_clock` | 1958-01-01 | International Atomic Time |
| `gps_clock` | 1980-01-06 UTC | GPS time |

`clock_cast<TargetClock>(tp)` converts between them:

```cpp
sys_seconds sys_tp = /*...*/;
utc_seconds utc_tp = clock_cast<utc_clock>(sys_tp);
gps_seconds gps_tp = clock_cast<gps_clock>(sys_tp);

// utc_clock and system_clock differ by accumulated leap seconds
// (27 as of 2026)
```

examples.title: Timezones: locate_zone, zoned_time, formatting
DST transitions and clock_cast
examples.expectedOutput:
before DST: 01:30 EST
after DST:  04:00 EDT
UTC - system_clock diff: 27 s (leap seconds)
GPS time_since_epoch: 1457006418 s

lessons.title: chrono in Practice: Measurement, parse, Formatting
lessons.motivation: Knowing the chrono types is only half the battle. Real code needs to: measure execution time (while choosing the right clock), parse a date from a string, find the difference between two dates, and determine the day of the week for an arbitrary date. C++20 closes the last gap: `std::chrono::parse` allows parsing a string into any chrono type using the same format specifiers that `std::format` uses for output.

examples.title: duration_cast, floor/ceil/round, date arithmetic
examples.expectedOutput:
floor to seconds: 1
ceil  to seconds: 2
round to seconds: 2
total seconds: 9015
total minutes: 150
event date: 2026-06-30
days since Jan 1: 180
weekday: Tue

examples.title: Parsing dates and practical helper functions
examples.expectedOutput:
parsed: 2026-12-31
parsed datetime: 2026-07-21 14:30:00
days until Dec 31: 163
Christmas 2026: Fri
benchmark: measured
